1: The Philistine oppression of forty years — the longest in Judges — frames the Samson narrative (chs13-16). The Philistines were one of the Sea Peoples who settled the Levantine coast around 1200 BCE following the collapse of the LBA palace economies. They established a pentapolis of five city-states: Gaza, Ashkelon, Ashdod, Ekron (Tel Miqne), and Gath — well-attested archaeologically by their distinctive Mycenaean-derived pottery (the Philistine bichrome ware), hearth-houses, and Aegean-style religious iconography. By Iron Age I the Philistines had become the dominant military power of the coastal plain, with iron technology giving them a significant military advantage (cf. 1 Sam 13:19-22: Israel had no iron tools; the Philistines controlled iron-working). The forty-year oppression reflects the full establishment of Philistine hegemony before Samson's one-man disruption of it.
2: Manoah of Zorah from the tribe of Dan — the geographic context is significant. Zorah (modern Sar'ah) and Eshtaol were Dan's territory in the foothills (Shephelah) between the coastal plain and the Judean highlands, directly on the border of Philistine territory. This was a contested zone: Dan's original allotment (Josh 19:40-48) had been largely unsuccessful because of Amorite and Philistine pressure (Judg 1:34 — 'the Amorites pressed the Danites back into the hill country'), which led to Dan's northern migration (ch18). The remaining Danites in the Zorah-Eshtaol area were living in the most dangerous frontier zone in Israel — the buffer between Philistia and the highlands. Manoah's barren wife will bear the man who will 'begin to save Israel from the hand of the Philistines' (v5).
3: The angel of YHWH's appearance to the unnamed wife of Manoah follows the pattern of divine birth announcements in Genesis: Sarah (Gen 18:10-14), Rebekah (Gen 25:23), and Rachel (Gen 30:22) all receive divine intervention in the context of barrenness. In the ancient Near East, barrenness was understood as a divine withholding of fertility — the womb was 'closed' by divine decision and could be 'opened' only by divine action (Gen 29:31; 30:22). The divine birth announcement to a barren woman is thus an act of sovereign creation: YHWH is not ratifying a natural pregnancy but initiating one that would not otherwise occur. The pattern is: barrenness → divine appearance → pregnancy → special child → deliverer. Samson is the last in this series before the most significant — John the Baptist and Jesus — in Luke 1.
4: The prenatal Nazirite instructions — 'now therefore be careful to drink no wine or strong drink, and eat nothing unclean' — extend the Nazirite restrictions to the mother during pregnancy. The Nazirite vow (Num 6:1-21) normally applied to an individual who voluntarily undertook it for a specific period; it involved abstention from wine and fermented drink, from shaving the head, and from contact with the dead. The prenatal application — which Num 6 does not mention — is unique to Samson and creates a Nazirite dedicated from conception. The mother's dietary restrictions reflect the ancient understanding that what the mother consumed affected the developing child — an intuition of prenatal influence that modern nutritional science has confirmed in principle.
5: 'For behold, you are with child and shall bear a son. No razor shall come on his head, for the child shall be a Nazirite to God from the womb, and he shall begin to save Israel from the hand of the Philistines.' The permanent Nazirite vow ('from the womb') is unique — even John the Baptist's prenatal dedication (Luke 1:15) uses similar language ('he will be filled with the Holy Spirit, even from his mother's womb'). The long hair of the Nazirite was the external sign of the vow's duration; cutting it would end the vow. That Samson's hair cannot be cut means the vow is unconditional and permanent — or rather, that Samson himself is the condition: when he violates the vow, the vow terminates. The phrase 'begin to save' (lāhāhel lĕhôšîaʿ) is unusual — YHWH will save Israel through Samson, but only begin to do so; the full deliverance will require David and the early monarchy.
6: The wife's report to her husband describes the angelic visitor as 'a man of God' (ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm) whose appearance was 'very awesome' or 'like the appearance of the angel of God' — she did not initially know he was the angel of YHWH. The term ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm (man of God) is normally used of prophets (Moses in Deut 33:1, Elijah in 1 Kgs 17:18, Elisha in 2 Kgs 4:7) — it designates a human figure who speaks with divine authority. Her description of his appearance as 'very awesome' (nôrāʾ məʾōd, H3372) suggests she perceived something numinous about him even if she could not identify him precisely.
7: The woman reports the angel's specific instructions — 'behold, you shall conceive and bear a son; now drink no wine or strong drink and eat nothing unclean, for the child shall be a Nazirite to God from the womb to the day of his death' — with the significant addition 'to the day of his death.' The permanent, lifelong Nazirite dedication 'until the day of his death' makes explicit what was implied in v5: there is no planned end to this vow. Ordinary Nazirites completed their vow and returned to normal life (Num 6:13-21). Samson's vow is permanent — his long hair is his entire identity as YHWH's instrument.
8: Manoah's prayer — 'O Lord, please let the man of God you sent come again to us and teach us what we shall do with the child who will be born' — is a request for divine instruction about child-rearing in the context of a divine commissioning. In the ancient Near East, divine birth announcements to kings (Egyptian royal birth scenes show the god forming the royal child, with instructions about the child's destiny) typically included guidance for the child's upbringing. Manoah's request follows this pattern: the divine commission includes not just the announcement but the instruction manual. The request is pious, though the angel's ultimate response (v12) will be to reiterate what he already said — the instructions have already been given.
9: God 'listened to the voice of Manoah' — the phrase wayyišmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm bəqôl mānôaḥ (God heard in the voice of Manoah) reflects the standard Hebrew idiom for answered prayer. The angel returns — but comes again to the wife alone, while she is sitting in the field and Manoah is not with her. This detail (repeated in v11: 'Manoah got up and followed his wife') consistently places the woman as the primary recipient of the divine communication, with Manoah as the secondary figure who has to be brought to the encounter. The gendered dynamics of the theophany are deliberate: YHWH chose to reveal himself to the woman, who then brings her husband into the encounter.
10: The woman ran quickly to tell her husband — her responsiveness to the divine messenger contrasts with Manoah's absence. The quick running to fetch Manoah mirrors Sarah's quick preparation (Gen 18:6) in the theophany at Mamre. In the social structure of the ancient household, the wife's role as the first to receive and transmit the divine communication inverts expected hierarchies — the husband is the head of household, but YHWH approaches through the wife. This pattern of divine communication through non-primary household members (younger sons, barren wives, women) is one of Judges' consistent theological notes.
11: Manoah's question to the returning angel — 'Are you the man who spoke to this woman?' — establishes continuity with the first appearance and begins the formal confirmation dialogue. The angel's 'I am' (ʾănî, simple first-person self-identification) is the minimal affirmation. Manoah's subsequent question (v12) — 'when your words come true, what is to be the boy's way of life, and what is his mission?' — asks for clarification of upbringing instructions. The distinction between 'way of life' (mišpāṭ hannaʿar, the custom/manner of the young man) and 'mission' (maʿăśēhû, his work/deed) reflects the ANE interest in both the rearing conditions and the purpose of a specially destined child.
12: Manoah's request for instruction 'when your words come true' is interesting: he is asking about the post-birth period, assuming the announcement will be fulfilled. The conditional grammar is not doubt but planning — 'when this happens, how do we raise him?' The angel's response (vv13-14) repeats what was already said to the woman: all the Nazirite restrictions apply. The repetition serves a confirmatory function — the husband receives the same instruction as the wife, creating double attestation. In ANE law, important transactions required witnesses and confirmation; the angel's repetition constitutes the second witnessing of the divine commission.
13: The angel's instruction to Manoah — 'of all that I said to the woman let her be careful' — places the mother as the primary responsible party for the prenatal restrictions. In the ancient household, the pregnant woman's dietary and behavioral restrictions were well-understood through practical medicine: ANE medical texts (the Diagnostic Handbook from Nineveh, Akkadian birth omens) document restrictions on pregnant women's food and activity. The instruction to the father that the mother must be careful is consistent with an ANE understanding of the mother as the agent responsible for the child's prenatal environment, with the father as the authority who must ensure her compliance.
14: The comprehensive Nazirite restrictions are restated: no grapevine products (from seeds to skins, not just wine), no fermented drink, no unclean food — but notably no explicit mention of the razor or the dead. The wine prohibition is the most elaborated aspect of the prenatal restriction, possibly because the consumption of wine in pregnancy was a common practice that needed the strongest prohibition. The expansion 'all that comes from the vine' (kol ʾăšer yeṣēʾ miggepen hayyayin) covers even grape seeds and skins — a more comprehensive prohibition than Num 6:4. This suggests that the Nazirite vow was being interpreted in its strictest sense for this uniquely permanent dedication.
15: Manoah's offer to prepare a young goat for the angel — 'let us detain you and prepare a young goat for you' — follows the standard hospitality protocol for divine or angelic visitors. The same offer is made to the three visitors at Mamre (Gen 18:7), to the angel at Gideon's theophany (6:19), and implicitly to the strangers at Lot's house (Gen 19:2-3). The hospitality meal is the ritual of welcome and reciprocity that precedes formal interaction. The angel's redirection of the meal from consumption to offering (v16) again parallels the Gideon theophany: the food prepared for a guest becomes a sacrifice.
16: The angel's refusal to eat — 'I will not eat of your bread, but if you prepare a burnt offering, offer it to YHWH' — reveals his true identity while maintaining the fiction of uncertainty. The clause 'for Manoah did not know that he was the angel of YHWH' is the narrator's aside confirming what the audience suspects. The refusal to eat mortal food is a consistent feature of ANE angelology: divine beings do not consume human food in the way humans do (cf. Gen 18:8 — the visitors at Mamre appear to eat, but the theophany is confirmed when they have human appearance but divine knowledge). The angel's direction of worship to YHWH rather than to himself anticipates the NT angel who refuses worship (Rev 19:10; 22:9).
17: Manoah's question — 'what is your name?' — follows a pattern in theophany narratives: Jacob asks the name of his wrestling opponent (Gen 32:29, 'tell me your name' — refused), Moses asks YHWH's name (Exod 3:13-14 — given with 'I am who I am'). Manoah's purpose is explicitly honorific: 'so that when your words come true, we may honor you' (wəkibbaḏnûkā, we will honor/glorify you). The request is for the name as the basis for future thanksgiving and honor — a legitimate pious motive. The refusal of the name that follows turns this act of seeking into an act of instruction: the angel's identity is not available for human honorification.
18: The angel's answer — 'why do you ask my name, seeing it is wonderful (pelîʾî)?' — is one of the most theologically pregnant phrases in the Samson narrative. Pelîʾ (H6383, wonderful/incomprehensible/beyond understanding) is a cognate of peleʾ (H6382, wonder/marvel) used in Isa 9:6: 'and his name shall be called Wonderful (peleʾ).' Whether the angel is saying his name IS 'Wonderful' or that his name is 'too wonderful to tell' (i.e., incomprehensible) is debated. In context, both readings are coherent: the angel's identity is identified with the incomprehensible divine name. The connection to Isa 9:6 suggests that later readers heard this as a pre-incarnate self-identification of the Son whose name is Wonderful.
19: Manoah's offering of a young goat and grain offering (minḥāh) on the rock — 'doing wonders' (mappilīʾ laʿăśôt, doing wondrously) as the angel ascends in the flame — creates the theophanic sign parallel to Gideon's encounter (6:21). The rock-altar and fire-from-rock is the consistent sign of the divine warrior's presence in Judges. The grain offering (minḥāh) alongside the animal sacrifice reflects the full burnt offering procedure of Lev 1-2: both components were standard at formal sacrifices. The 'doing wonders' language connects this to the Exodus tradition of YHWH's 'wonders' (niplāʾôt) — the theophany at Zorah is presented as a miniature Sinai-event.
20: The angel ascending in the flame of the altar — 'it happened as the flame went up from the altar toward heaven that the angel of YHWH went up in the flame' — is the clearest statement in Judges of the angel's divine identity. Fire as the medium of divine ascent recalls the pillar of fire in the wilderness (Exod 13:21-22) and the theophany to Moses in the burning bush (Exod 3:2). The angel does not walk away; he ascends — vertically, in fire, toward heaven. Manoah and his wife 'fell on their faces to the ground' — the prostration response to divine encounter that runs from Abraham (Gen 17:3) through the Gospels (Matt 17:6) to Revelation (Rev 1:17).
21: 'The angel of YHWH appeared no more to Manoah and to his wife' — the statement of finality marks the end of the theophanic series. The angel's mission was accomplished: the birth announcement was given, confirmed, and the offering received. The cessation of divine appearances after the mission's completion is consistent across Judges theophanies: the angel appears, delivers the commission, and departs. The verse then makes explicit what has been implicit: 'Then Manoah knew that he was the angel of YHWH' — the recognition arrives only after the departure, confirming the post-hoc pattern of theophanic recognition in Judges (cf. Gideon's recognition in 6:22).
22: Manoah's fear — 'We shall surely die, for we have seen God' — is the standard theophany-terror response, grounded in the ancient belief that full divine encounter was fatal (Exod 33:20). The word 'God' (ʾĕlōhîm) rather than 'angel of YHWH' in Manoah's statement shows his theological resolution: the one who ascended in the altar fire was not a messenger but God himself. Manoah has correctly identified the nature of the encounter. His wife's counterargument (v23) will work by a different logical register: not 'you are wrong about who it was' but 'if he intended to kill us, the evidence does not support that intention.'
23: The wife's theological argument is a model of practical reasoning from divine consistency: 'If YHWH had meant to kill us, he would not have accepted a burnt offering and grain offering at our hands, or shown us all these things, or now announced to us such things as these.' The argument has three elements: (1) YHWH accepted our sacrifice — divine acceptance of sacrifice implies relationship, not judgment; (2) YHWH showed us wonders — wonders are displays of divine grace, not harbingers of death; (3) YHWH gave us a birth announcement — a promise of future blessing is incompatible with a plan to immediately kill. This is covenantal reasoning: the pattern of YHWH's past actions in this encounter is inconsistent with a plan for their death. The wife reasons better than her husband.
24: The birth and naming of Samson — 'the woman bore a son and called his name Samson, and the young man grew, and YHWH blessed him' — is terse compared to the elaborate birth announcements. Šimšôn (Samson, H8123) is related to šemeš (sun, H8121) — possibly 'sunny one' or 'solar' — which has generated much scholarly discussion about possible solar mythology underlying the Samson traditions. Whether the name has cosmological freight or is simply a personal name derived from a common noun, the connection to the sun (which rises and sets, whose strength waxes and wanes) has been noted by interpreters from antiquity. YHWH's blessing marks the child as set apart from birth for divine purposes.
25: 'The Spirit of YHWH began to stir him (palʿam) in Mahaneh-dan, between Zorah and Eshtaol' — the verb pāʿam (H6470, to thrust/disturb/stir — used of inner agitation or impulse) describes not the full rushing-upon empowerment of battle (which uses ṣālaḥ) but a preliminary stirring, a divine restlessness that begins to manifest in Samson. Mahaneh-dan ('camp of Dan,' also mentioned in 18:12 as a staging point for the Danite migration) was in the Shephelah between Samson's hometown Zorah and Eshtaol — the very border region of Philistine and Israelite territory. The Spirit's stirring begins in the most contested geographic space, preparing Samson for his boundary-crossing, rule-breaking, culture-challenging mission against the Philistines.
